metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the sub-case (b) policy to specify the child-level STATUS fork outcome: if both clones claim the same child, the child's STATUS fork is resolved by the same UUID tie-break independently of the parent fork; document whether the test scenario uses the same-child (both race on one child) or different-children (each claims its own child, so no child STATUS fork occurs) and align the 'orphaned' wording accordingly.
findings.finding: Sub-case (b) policy and test assertions omit the child-level STATUS fork. When both offline clones each claim parent+child, there are concurrent STATUS events on both the parent AND the child. The reducer's UUID tie-break resolves both forks independently. The plan only specifies the parent's STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED as the losing-agent signal and says 'the losing agent's child claim is left in place (orphaned under the losing assignee)' — but the child's own STATUS fork resolution may also revert the child to 'open' (if the losing UUID also loses the child fork), which is a materially different outcome from 'in_progress orphaned under a different assignee'. The policy statement and test for sub-case (b) must address the child STATUS fork outcome.
findings.location: What section / sub-case (b) policy + AC: 'Two-clone offline race test (sub-case b)'
findings.suggested_fix: Clarify the sub-case (b) scenario so the two clones claim DIFFERENT children (so only the parent STATUS forks, not the children's), which makes the 'orphaned under the losing assignee' claim exact and testable. Alternatively, if both clones claim the same child, add a policy statement covering the child's STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED outcome.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the policy doc and sub-case (b) test that the authoritative losing-agent signal is the merged EDIT/assignee field (per I8: 'the merged assignee — the ownership authority — is not the claimant'), not solely STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED; STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED is a corroborating audit signal that fires only when both STATUS events race from the same 'open' current_status, while the EDIT/assignee check is always definitive.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that in sub-case (b), 'the parent's assignee is resolved by the normal STATUS/HLC fork resolution (surfaced as STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED)' and that 'the losing agent signal is the parent's STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED'. Code inspection shows claim_core writes two events — STATUS(in_progress) via UUID-lower-wins fork resolution, and EDIT(assignee) via HLC-last-writer-wins. These are independent mechanisms. The authoritative ownership signal per I8 in docs/concurrency.md is the merged EDIT/assignee field ('the merged assignee — the ownership authority — is not the claimant'), not STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED. The plan's description of STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED as the losing-agent signal is therefore partly incorrect or incomplete: STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED fires only when two STATUS events fork from the same current_status (both clones see parent as 'open'), but if pushes happen to serialize the STATUS events won't fork and STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED won't appear, while the EDIT/assignee mismatch remains the reliable loss indicator.
findings.location: Plan section 'What', sub-case (b); AC line 'The losing-agent signal is the parent's STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED'
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section and AC, replace 'The losing-agent signal is the parent's STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED' with: 'The losing agent detects loss via TWO signals: (1) the merged assignee field on the parent is not the losing clone's assignee (the authoritative ownership authority per I8); (2) STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED is emitted when both STATUS events raced from the same open current_status (may not appear if pushes serialize). The AC test must assert (1) always and (2) when the true offline-simultaneous race is reproduced.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the AC for sub-case (b), specify the exact observable schema element (e.g., `assignee` field on the child ticket, a new `orphaned_parent_claim` derived state key, or a named fsck finding kind) that a test must read to assert 'the losing agent's child claim remains in place (not rolled back).'
findings.finding: The plan introduces an 'orphaned child claim' state (sub-case b: losing agent's child claim left in place after parent STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED) but never defines the observable schema element by which a test or executor can detect and assert this outcome — neither a state field, a derived-state key, nor a fsck finding kind is named or specified.
findings.location: What section (sub-case b) and AC: 'Two-clone offline race test (sub-case b)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the sub-case (b) AC: 'Assert this by checking that the child ticket's `assignee` field (from `rebar show <child>`) still equals the losing agent's assignee after merge, and that no rollback event exists in the child's event log.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify sub-case (a) test setup: either name the new fixture needed for a same-tracker/two-process race (e.g. two concurrent subprocesses sharing one `tmp_path` tracker), or correct the AC to reflect that sub-case (a) is a sequential single-process test (agent A then B, serialized by the write lock), which would require no new fixture but also tests a weaker property.
findings.finding: Sub-case (a) specifies 'two real processes race on one tracker' (same tracker, not two separate clones), but the only reusable fixture in the target file (`two_clones`) provisions two separate clone directories sharing a remote — suitable for sub-case (b). A same-tracker two-process test requires a different fixture setup (two subprocess workers pointing at the same tracker path with shared locking). This new fixture is not named or described in the plan, leaving the implementer without a specified harness for sub-case (a).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Same-tracker process race test (sub-case a)' / tests/integration/test_concurrency_regression.py

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test assertion (or a second test case) for sub-case (a) where agent B races while the parent is still OPEN and loses, confirming that B's child claim is never written.
findings.finding: The test for sub-case (a) covers only the serialized-success path (B sees an already in_progress parent and claims its child independently). The plan's own policy explicitly describes a second outcome — B arrives while the parent is still OPEN, loses the parent-claim race, and its child is NEVER claimed — but no AC or test description covers this loss/failure branch.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Same-tracker process race test (sub-case a)'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the sub-case (a) test to include a scenario where both agents start before either has claimed the parent (parent is still OPEN), B loses the race, and then assert that B's child task remains unclaimed (no claim record written for B's child).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the RETIRE path for orphaned child claims in `docs/concurrency.md`: specify who is responsible for closing/cancelling them (e.g., the losing agent on detecting `STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED`, a human operator, or an automated GC step), and add an AC verifying this is exercised in the sub-case (b) test.
findings.finding: The orphaned child claim produced by sub-case (b) has no defined RETIRE/cleanup lifecycle path. The plan explicitly states it is 'NOT retroactively rolled back or tombstoned,' but never specifies who eventually closes, cancels, or expires the orphaned claim, nor under what conditions. This means orphaned child claims accumulate indefinitely in the tracker with no resolution path.
findings.location: ## What — sub-case (b) description; Acceptance Criteria — 'losing agent's child claim remains in place (not rolled back)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a policy clause: 'On detecting STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED on the parent, the losing agent SHOULD cancel or abandon its orphaned child claim (transition to `cancelled` with a note referencing the fork resolution event). If the agent is offline/dead, a reconciliation pass marks orphaned children as `cancelled` after a configurable TTL.' Encode this as an additional assertion in the sub-case (b) test.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Commit `docs/concurrency.md` as the *canonical* location for the parent-cascade race policy (not 'and/or'); update CLAUDE.md to reference it rather than duplicate or potentially replace it.
findings.finding: The Scope and AC use 'docs/concurrency.md and/or CLAUDE.md' ambiguously — if the implementation chooses only CLAUDE.md (an agent-instruction file), the architectural decision about orphaned child claims and STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED semantics would have no canonical developer-facing design document, making it hard for human contributors to discover the policy.
findings.location: Scope section: 'docs/concurrency.md and/or CLAUDE.md (policy statement)'; AC line 1
findings.suggested_fix: Change the Scope line to: 'docs/concurrency.md (canonical policy doc) — CLAUDE.md references this file'. Update AC line 1 to require the policy be present in docs/concurrency.md specifically.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State a default canonical file for the policy (e.g. 'create `docs/concurrency.md`; add a one-line reference from CLAUDE.md') so AC1 has a single verifiable location.
findings.finding: The documentation target is stated as 'docs/concurrency.md and/or CLAUDE.md' in both the What section and AC1, leaving it indeterminate which file(s) to create or update. An executor must guess, and a reviewer checking AC1 has no single canonical location to inspect.
findings.location: AC1 / 'What' section: 'docs/concurrency.md and/or CLAUDE.md'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'docs/concurrency.md and/or CLAUDE.md' with 'docs/concurrency.md (add a cross-reference link in CLAUDE.md)' throughout the plan and AC1.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify the specific function/API call (e.g. module path and function name) used to simulate the offline-clone merge in tests so the executor can write sub-case (b) without codebase archaeology.
findings.finding: The two-clone offline race test (sub-case b) requires invoking the merge/sync mechanism that triggers STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED, but the plan does not identify the API, function, or helper the test must call to simulate the offline-clone merge. An executor unfamiliar with the codebase cannot write this test without a discovery step.
findings.location: AC3 / 'What' sub-case (b): 'Two offline clones … then merge'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability requirement to the policy doc and ACs: STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED events and resulting orphaned child claims must be logged at WARN level (or equivalent structured log) with the parent task ID, winning assignee, and losing assignee, so operators can detect and act on them.
findings.finding: No observability is specified for the two new exceptional states introduced: (1) orphaned child claims and (2) STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED events on parents. Operators have no instrumented signal (log line, metric, alert) to detect that a fork resolution occurred or that orphaned claims exist, making silent accumulation undetectable in production.
findings.location: ## What — sub-case (b); Acceptance Criteria bullet 3
findings.suggested_fix: In docs/concurrency.md, add: 'Implementations MUST emit a structured log entry at WARN level whenever STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED is applied, including: parent_task_id, winning_assignee, losing_assignee, orphaned_child_task_ids.' Add a corresponding AC: 'The sub-case (b) test asserts that a WARN-level log entry is emitted containing the expected fields.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the AC that the offline-merge test uses the real merge/reconciliation mechanism (not a simulated state injection), or explicitly document why a substitute is faithful to production.
findings.finding: The two-clone offline race test (sub-case b) describes 'both clones claim parent+child offline, then merge' but does not specify whether the merge step exercises the real merge/convergence mechanism or a simulated substitute. If the merge is a stub or an in-memory fake, the test could pass green while the actual on-disk merge path is broken — a proxy-validation risk.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Two-clone offline race test (sub-case b)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC: 'The merge step in sub-case (b) must invoke the actual tracker merge/reconciliation path (e.g., real git merge or equivalent DB sync), not a hand-crafted state injection, so that STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED is emitted by the live code path.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence positive rationale to the policy statement explaining why no-rollback is the chosen approach (e.g., 'no rollback because appending a compensating event would require the losing agent to know it lost before it can write, which conflicts with the offline/async model; the STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED signal is sufficient for the losing agent to self-detect and stop').
findings.finding: The plan states no positive rationale for choosing the no-rollback / orphaned-child-claim policy over the structurally different alternative of appending a REVERT (or similar compensating) event on the child when the parent-claim loss is detected post-merge. Without a stated rationale, implementers cannot evaluate whether a future need (e.g. an agent that needs a clean signal that its child claim is invalid, not just 'check the parent's STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED') warrants revisiting the approach.
findings.location: What section / committed policy description

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a co-located decision rule to the sub-case (b) policy (in `docs/concurrency.md`) specifying what the losing agent should do upon detecting `STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED` on its parent — e.g., 'the losing agent SHOULD abandon the child or coordinate with the winning agent; continuing to work the child is undefined behavior under this policy.'
findings.finding: The plan names the parent's STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED as the detection signal for sub-case (b) but provides no co-located decision rule for what the losing agent should do with its orphaned child claim upon detecting that signal — the response protocol is absent, leaving an instruction-locality gap for agent consumers of the documented policy.
findings.location: What section (sub-case b): 'The losing-agent signal is the parent's STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED, from which an agent can detect it no longer owns the parent.'

findings.finding: AC1 introduces the term 'losing parent claim is resolved after a merge' as a decision rule to be documented, but provides no vocabulary for what 'resolved' means in this context — whether the losing agent's *child* claim is also invalidated (tombstoned, rolled back, left orphaned), or left in place. This decision rule is referenced by AC3's test but is not co-located with the existing `ConcurrencyMismatch` / `STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED` schema that a test would need to assert against.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 ('how a losing parent claim is resolved after a merge')

findings.finding: The plan's AC-3 says the two-clone test should 'assert the documented losing-agent signal after merge,' but claim_compute's push_after_commit loss detection fires only for the specific ticket just committed. In the same-parent/different-child scenario there are two distinct sub-cases: (a) B's parent claim loses on merge before B attempts its child claim — claim_compute's fail-fast cascade means B never claims child-2 at all; (b) B's parent claim wins locally (A's was not yet pushed) and B claims child-2, but the parent's assignee fork resolves against B after merge. The plan does not specify which sub-case the test should exercise, so an implementer could construct either scenario and assert only half the policy semantics. The documentation step (AC-1) likewise needs to call out both sub-cases to be complete.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC-1 and AC-3 (two-clone offline race test)

findings.finding: The plan's core premise — that the ownership policy for same-parent/different-child races is 'not written down' — conflicts with existing prior art in docs/concurrency.md. I4a explicitly states that a parent already `in_progress`/`closed`/`blocked` 'is **not** cascaded — only the requested ticket moves', directly answering whether children may be claimed independently once the parent is in_progress. I8 already documents the losing-claim signal ('claim lost on cross-clone merge', exit 10/ConcurrencyMismatch). Writing a new policy statement without anchoring to or extending these canonical invariants risks producing a redundant or contradicting definition.
findings.location: ## What / ## Acceptance Criteria AC1 / docs/concurrency.md I4a, I8
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's ## What section, replace 'Decide and document the same-parent/different-child ownership policy' with 'Verify docs/concurrency.md I4a/I8 covers the same-parent/different-child race semantics; add a focused cross-reference or extension (e.g. a new I4b) for any gap — specifically whether a parent's losing cross-clone claim invalidates an already-committed child claim — rather than writing a standalone policy that could conflict with I4a.'

findings.finding: AC3 references asserting 'the documented losing-agent signal after merge' but neither the plan nor the policy (which doesn't yet exist) defines what form this signal takes — exception, return code, status field value, log message — leaving the executor unable to write a concrete assertion without stopping to ask.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC3: 'losing-agent signal is defined as [e.g. the task's `status` field being set to `conflict` or an `AgentConflictError` exception]; assert `task.status == "conflict"` (or equivalent) in the test.'

findings.finding: The plan defers the core design decision ('Decide and document the same-parent/different-child ownership policy') entirely to the executor with no stated default or constrained option set, making it a placeholder that blocks writing AC2 and AC3 assertions until after an unreviewed design choice is made.
findings.location: What section / Scope bullet: 'Decide and document the same-parent/different-child ownership policy'

findings.finding: The RETIRE/cleanup path for a losing agent's child claim after merge is never specified. The AC says 'asserts the documented losing-agent signal after merge' but does not define what constitutes a valid retirement action (claim revocation, status reset, error returned to agent, orphaned assignee left in place), leaving the lifecycle incomplete and the test assertion unverifiable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Two-clone offline race test … asserts the documented losing-agent signal after merge'
findings.suggested_fix: In the AC, replace 'asserts the documented losing-agent signal after merge' with a concrete outcome, e.g.: 'asserts that the losing agent's child claim is revoked (assignee reset, status returned to `open`) and that the agent receives a deterministic ConflictError or equivalent sentinel, as defined in docs/concurrency.md §Losing-Claim Resolution'.

findings.finding: AC3 references 'asserts the documented losing-agent signal after merge' but neither the plan nor any existing documentation defines what the 'losing-agent signal' is in the new same-parent/different-child cross-clone race scenario. The single-ticket same-clone losing signal (exit 10 / `ConcurrencyMismatch`, message 'claim lost on cross-clone merge') is defined in `src/rebar/_commands/claim.py:190` and `docs/concurrency.md` I8, but whether that same signal applies when a parent claim loses — or whether a new signal (a new error type, a STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED path, or a child-orphan exit) is warranted — is left entirely to the policy document that the plan says must be written. A test asserting an undefined signal cannot be authored.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 ('asserts the documented losing-agent signal after merge')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC1 (the policy statement) an explicit enumeration of the losing-agent signal for each sub-case: (a) agent B's parent claim races and loses before child is attempted → already exit 10 (I4 optimistic concurrency); (b) agent B's parent claim wins locally but loses on post-push merge → existing 'claim lost on cross-clone merge' exit 10; (c) agent B successfully claimed a child but the parent assignee is later overwritten by merge → STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED + fsck report (no automatic exit 10 since the child op already completed). AC3 can then name which case it tests.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
